topic: Two siblings inherit their grandmother's house
raw_output:
[story 1]

The solicitor’s crisp documents felt like sandpaper against my skin. I stared at the deed, the ink blurring into meaningless black smudges. My hands began to tremble uncontrollably, a fine vibration running up my arms. "This is impossible," I whispered, the sound thin and reedy in the cavernous entryway. The air in the old house seemed too thick, pressing down on my chest until breathing became a conscious, labored effort. I paced the parquet floor, my heels clicking a frantic, uneven rhythm. Every shadow in the corners seemed to writhe, and I couldn't focus on the details; all I could see were flashing, disjointed images of the inheritance, the weight of it all crushing my lungs. I needed air, but the space felt suffocating, and my throat tightened with a desperate need to scream.

[story 2]

My brother was already downstairs, downstairs sorting through dusty trinkets, humming some tuneless melody. I stood by the grand staircase, my knuckles white where I gripped the banister. A sudden, sharp intake of breath caught in my throat, followed by a dizzying lurch. The sheer volume of expectation—the responsibility, the sudden shift in my entire world—was overwhelming. My vision tunneled; the wallpaper seemed to ripple, and the silence of the house amplified the frantic hammering of my own heart against my ribs. I started pacing in tight, jerky circles, my gaze darting everywhere and nowhere. "It can't be real," I muttered, my voice cracking on the last word. I pressed the heels of my palms against my eyes, trying to physically block out the overwhelming sensory input, but the world continued to spin, colors bleeding together into a nauseating smear.

[story 3]

I couldn't sit still. The sheer audacity of the situation—this crumbling, magnificent mausoleum now mine—was unraveling my composure thread by thread. I began to pace the length of the drawing-room, my movements becoming sharp, almost violent. My breath hitched in short, shallow gasps, and a strange, hot flush spread across my neck and face. I kept touching my temples, as if trying to physically contain the buzzing energy inside my skull. "Stop," I managed to choke out, the word sounding foreign even to my own ears. The antique furniture seemed to lean in, the draperies rustled with an unnatural insistence. I spun around, my eyes wide and unfocused, searching for an anchor in the reality that was dissolving beneath me, leaving only a chaotic, vibrating rush.
